1→# Project 2: M3ssag1n8
     2→
     3→For this project, you will build a web-based messaging application, similar to Discord, Slack, etc., using HTML, CSS, and TypeScript. Your COMP 318 m3ssag1n8 application will be an independent web client that can be served as static resources from any web server. For the dynamic messaging content, you will use OwlDB as the back-end web service to store all the application's messages and other information. Note that this means that the functionality and behavior of your application will be limited by the capabilities of OwlDB. In other words, if something cannot be done in the browser or by using OwlDB, then your messaging application will not be able to incorporate that feature.
     4→
     5→Throughout this document, if you see the word **must** then that means this is a requirement of the project that you must do. If you see the word **should**, then that means you *should* follow the given advice, but you do not have to. If you choose not to, however, you could lose points if you use an inferior method.
     6→
     7→Note that this document describes the requirements of the project. By default, everything is a requirement (whether you see the word "must" or not) unless specified otherwise (using "should" or some other clear statement). The word "must" is used to emphasize particularly important requirements and/or constraints.
     8→
     9→## Table of Contents
    10→
    11→1. [Application Overview](#application-overview)
    12→2. [Database Structure](#database-structure)
    13→3. [User Interface](#user-interface)
    14→4. [Layout and Styling](#layout-and-styling)
    15→5. [Accessibility](#accessibility)
    16→6. [Application Extensions](#application-extensions)
    17→7. [Icons](#icons)
    18→8. [Validation](#validation)
    19→9. [Constraints](#constraints)
    20→10. [Design](#design)
    21→11. [Concurrency](#concurrency)
    22→12. [Testing](#testing)
    23→13. [Deliverables](#deliverables)
    24→14. [Design Document](#design-document)
    25→15. [Grading](#grading)
    26→
    27→## Application Overview
    28→
    29→You will build a web-based messaging application similar to Discord, Slack, etc. Your application must have the following elements:
    30→
    31→1. A user must log in to use the application. Logging in only requires a username, not a password.
    32→2. Once logged in, there is a set of zero or more *workspaces*. Users can create, view, and delete workspaces. These same workspaces should be visible to all users who log in. In other words, there is no segregation of users: all users can see all workspaces.
    33→3. Each workspace is a set of zero or more *channels*. Users can create, view, and delete channels.
    34→4. Each channel is a set of zero or more *posts*. Users can create and view posts. Posts cannot be deleted. There are also additional features of posts that will be described below.
    35→
    36→Your application does not need to look exactly like this, but the following figure should give you a feel for the different elements of the application.
    37→
    38→Notice the following elements:
    39→
    40→1. There is a "logout" link. When no one is logged in, the user should not be able to interact with the interface in any way other than to log in.
    41→2. The currently logged-in user name is displayed.
    42→3. There is a mechanism to allow the user to select a workspace (in this case, by clicking the "workspaces" button that pops up a dialog allowing users to create, delete, or open a workspace - you do not need to match this behavior).
    43→4. The open workspace name (COMP 318) is displayed along with a list of channels in that workspace.
    44→5. The user can create, delete, or open a channel.
    45→6. The open channel name (Project 2) is displayed.
    46→7. The posts within the open channel are displayed.
    47→
    48→In an actual messaging application, users would likely have different permissions controlling who can access and/or delete specific resources. To simplify this project, you do not need to have such authorization mechanisms.
    49→
    50→The elements described above and their requirements will be further clarified below.
    51→
    52→## Database Structure
    53→
    54→All application state must be stored in a single OwlDB database. Your client must be able to interoperate with any other correctly built client that obeys this storage structure. You must not store application state any other way.
    55→
    56→You must validate all data you receive from OwlDB using appropriate JSON schemas. This will "protect" your client from malformed data written to the database by other misbehaving clients.
    57→
    58→Three main elements must be stored in the database:
    59→
    60→1. Each *workspace* should be a document in the top level of the database that contains a single collection called *channels*.
    61→2. Each *channel* in a workspace should be a document in the *channels* collection of the workspace with a single collection called *posts*.
    62→3. Each *post* in a channel should be a document in the *posts* collection of the channel.
    63→
    64→The contents of each *workspace* document must be a JSON object. As the only thing you need to know about a workspace is its name, this JSON object will typically be empty ("{}"). However, extensions may each add one additional property (that is the extension's name) to the JSON object, so you must not require/enforce that it is empty, just that it is a valid JSON object. You should ignore properties in the object for extensions you do not know about.
    65→
    66→The contents of each *channel* document must be a JSON object. As the only thing you need to know about a channel is its name, this JSON object will typically be empty ("{}"). However, extensions may each add one additional property (that is the extension's name) to the JSON object, so you must not require/enforce that it is empty, just that it is a valid JSON object. You should ignore properties in the object for extensions you do not know about.
    67→
    68→The contents of a *post* document must be a JSON object with the following properties:
    69→
    70→1. "msg" (required): the value of this property must be an arbitrary string, which is the post's content.
    71→2. "parent" (optional): if this message is a top-level post in the channel, this field must either be missing or have an empty string ("") as its value. If this message is a reply to another post (at any level), this field must be a string that gives the document path (this is the path within the database, so it should not include the database name).
    72→3. "reactions" (optional): the value of this property (if it exists) must be a JSON object. The JSON object must map strings corresponding to reaction names to JSON arrays. These JSON arrays must contain strings that correspond to user names. If the "reactions" property does not exist in the document, then no one has reacted to this post in any way. If a property in the JSON object maps to an empty JSON array, then no one has currently selected that reaction.
    73→4. "extensions" (optional): the value of this property (if it exists) must be a JSON object. Extensions may each optionally add one property to this JSON object. The property name must be the name of the extension. The value of each extension's property is defined by that extension. You should ignore properties in this object for extensions you do not know about.
    74→
    75→Note that there are *many* other ways you could store messaging data in OwlDB. However, you **must** store the data in this way.
    76→
    77→## User Interface
    78→
    79→As described in the application overview, the user interface must support the following capabilities:
    80→
    81→1. Login/logout
    82→2. Workspace selection, creation, and removal
    83→3. Channel selection, creation, and removal
    84→4. View and react to all posts within a channel
    85→5. Formatting posts
    86→6. Creating and replying to posts
    87→
    88→Workspace/channel lists do not need to update automatically (but users must have a button or other mechanism to update them). Posts must be updated in real-time. Note that this means you must subscribe to the currently open channel in OwlDB.
    89→
    90→Your user interface should be developed in a modular and encapsulated way using web components. The following sections provide more detail on the above six elements.
    91→
    92→### 1. Login/Logout
    93→
    94→Your application **must** use a modal dialog to force people to log in. Once a user has been logged in, there should be a clear and obvious way to log out. Once the logout is complete, the modal dialog to log in must reappear. Note that a "modal dialog" is a dialog box on top of the page that is the only element of the page that the user can interact with. While it is displayed, the rest of the page remains visible in the background, but they cannot interact with any other part of the page until they have closed the modal dialog (in this case, closing it should only be possible by successfully logging in). You should blur the background behind the modal dialog to indicate to the user that they cannot yet interact with it.
    95→
    96→To log in, you must use OwlDB's authentication methods to acquire a bearer token, which must then be used for all future interactions with OwlDB until the user logs out. When they log out, you must alert OwlDB so that the token will be destroyed. As we do not have an encrypted way to communicate with OwlDB, and OwlDB only requires a username to log in, you will only need to ask the user for a username, not a password. **In any actual system, you would need an encrypted way of exchanging and verifying credentials (such as passwords).** Note, however, that the bearer token authorization mechanisms you will use in your application would remain the same even if you had mechani

   238→}

   240→/**

   243→export function cleanupWorkspace(

   250→/**
   251→ * Utility: get the position (index among siblings) of an element
   252→ * containing the given text, for verifying ordering.
   253→ */
   254→export function getPostOrder(
   255→  texts: string[],
   256→): Cypress.Chainable<number[]> {
   257→  const positions: number[] = [];
   258→  const chain = cy.wrap(null);
   259→  for (const text of texts) {
   260→    chain.then(() => {
   261→      return cy
   262→        .contains(text)
   263→        .then(($el) => {
   264→          // Get the element's position relative to the document
   265→          const rect = $el[0].getBoundingClientRect();
   266→          positions.push(rect.top);
   267→        });
   268→    });
   269→  }
   270→  return chain.then(() => positions);
   271→}

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the project. Let me plan the implementation:

   - Workspace: `{DATABASE_PATH}{workspace_name}` (PUT to create)
   - Channel: `{DATABASE_PATH}{workspace}/channels/{channel}` (PUT to create)
   - Post: `{DATABASE_PATH}{workspace}/channels/{channel}/posts/` (POST to create)

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive response about the OwlDB REST API response format.

## OwlDB REST API Response Format Summary

Based on my thorough examination of the instructions, test files, and test harness, here's the complete API response format documentation:

### **1. GET Document Response** (`GET /v1/{database}/{documentPath}`)
**Status Code:** 200 OK
**Response Body:**
```json
{
  "path": "string",
  "doc": {
    "additionalProp1": "string",
    "additionalProp2": "string",
    "additionalProp3": "string"
  },
  "meta": {
    "createdAt": 0,
    "createdBy": "string",
    "lastModifiedAt": 0,
    "lastModifiedBy": "string"
  }
}
```

**Metadata Fields:**
- `createdAt`: Unix timestamp in milliseconds (number)
- `createdBy`: Username (string)
- `lastModifiedAt`: Unix timestamp in milliseconds (number)
- `lastModifiedBy`: Username (string)

---

### **2. GET Collection Response** (`GET /v1/{database}/{collectionPath}/` or `GET /v1/{database}/`)
**Status Code:** 200 OK
**Response Body:** JSON Array of document objects:
```json
[
  {
    "path": "string",
    "doc": {
      "additionalProp1": "string",
      "additionalProp2": "string",
      "additionalProp3": "string"
    },
    "meta": {
      "createdAt": 0,
      "createdBy": "string",
      "lastModifiedAt": 0,
      "lastModifiedBy": "string"
    }
  }
]
```

Each element in the array is a complete document object with path, doc, and meta fields. The array can be empty for empty collections.

---

### **3. PUT Document Response** (`PUT /v1/{database}/{documentPath}`)
**Status Codes:**
- **201 Created** (when document created)
- **200 OK** (when document replaced)

**Response Body:**
```json
{
  "uri": "/v1/db/doc"
}
```

**Headers:**
- `Location`: Relative URI of the document (e.g., `/v1/db/doc`)

---

### **4. POST to Collection (Auto-generate name)** (`POST /v1/{database}/` or `POST /v1/{database}/{collectionPath}/`)
**Status Code:** 201 Created
**Response Body:**
```json
{
  "uri": "/v1/db/a27czPEcQ0l3oS"
}
```

The server generates a unique document ID for the document within the collection. The response includes the full URI where the document was created.

**Headers:**
- `Location`: Relative URI of the created document

---

### **5. PATCH Document Response** (`PATCH /v1/{database}/{documentPath}`)
**Status Code:** 200 OK (always, whether patch succeeds or fails)
**Response Body:**
```json
{
  "uri": "/v1/db/doc",
  "patchFailed": false,
  "message": "patches applied"
}
```

**Fields:**
- `uri`: Full path to the patched document
- `patchFailed`: Boolean - `false` if all patches applied successfully, `true` if any patch failed
- `message`: String providing information:
  - Success: `"patches applied"`
  - Failure: Descriptive error message explaining why the patch failed

**Important:** Even if the patch fails, status code is still 200 OK. Check `patchFailed` field in response body.

**Headers:**
- `Location`: Relative URI of the document

---

### **6. PUT/DELETE Database Response**
**PUT Database (201 Created):**
```json
{
  "uri": "/v1/my_new_database"
}
```

**DELETE Database (204 No Content):**
- Empty response body

**Headers (PUT):**
- `Location`: Relative URI of the database

---

### **7. Login Response** (`POST /auth`)
**Status Code:** 200 OK
**Response Body:**
```json
{
  "token": "a88dBdX3z9kl3Q"
}
```

---

### **8. SSE Subscription Event Format** (`GET /v1/{path}?mode=subscribe`)

**Update Event Format:**
```
event: update
data: {"path":"/path/to/document","doc":{...},"meta":{...}}
id: 199834388899
```

The `data` field contains the same structure as a regular GET document response (with `path`, `doc`, and `meta` fields).

**Delete Event Format:**
```
event: delete
data: "/path/to/document-or-collection"
id: 19983438883
```

The `data` field is a quoted JSON string containing only the path.

**Event ID:** Unix timestamp in milliseconds

**Keepalive:** Server should send comment lines (`:`) every ~15 seconds to keep connection alive:
```
: keepalive comment
```

---

### **9. Key Points About Response Structure**

**Response Wrapping:**
- GET document: Response is wrapped with `"path"`, `"doc"`, and `"meta"` fields
- GET collection: Returns array where each element contains `"path"`, `"doc"`, and `"meta"` fields
- Tests show code that checks both wrapped (`result["doc"]`) and unwrapped (`result["field"]`) formats for flexibility

**Metadata Timestamps:**
- All timestamps are Unix milliseconds (not seconds)
- Field names: `createdAt`, `createdBy`, `lastModifiedAt`, `lastModifiedBy`

**Status Codes:**
- Document operations: 200 (success on existing), 201 (created)
- Collection operations: 200 (get), 201 (create), 204 (delete)
- Errors: 400 (bad request), 401 (unauthorized), 404 (not found), 409 (conflict), 412 (precondition failed)

**PATCH Semantics:**
- Always returns 200 even if patch fails
- Check `patchFailed` boolean to determine success
- Documents are NOT modified if ANY patch operation fails (atomic)

**Location Headers:**
- Returned on creation/modification endpoints
- Contains relative URI path (e.g., `/v1/db/doc`)

---

Once you have a working skip list, you can then easily just replace your initial implementation without changing any code that uses the index.

   530→### GET /v1/{database}/ - Get all documents in the database's top-level collection
   531→
   532→Retrieves the documents within the top-level collection of the database with the name given in the URL. If the interval query parameter is not present, or it is set to [,], then all documents in the collection will be returned. If the interval query parameter is set to an interval [low,high] only the documents with names between low and high, inclusive, will be returned. If the lower or upper bound is omitted (i.e., [low,]), that means there is no bound in the omitted direction. If the mode query parameter is set to subscribe, then this is an SSE request and all documents in the given interval will be sent as events, as will any future updates in the interval. Returns an error if the database is not found or the query parameters are malformed. Also returns an error if there is not a valid bearer token in the Authorization header.

   833→### PATCH /v1/{database}/{documentPath} - Modify document

151→## Subscribing to Changes
   152→
   153→A useful feature of a cloud database is to allow clients to "subscribe" to updates. Your system must support client subscriptions to a single document or a single collection. If a client is subscribed to a document, they must be notified if that document is modified (changed or completely overwritten) or deleted. If a client is subscribed to a collection, they must be notified if a document in the collection is modified (changed or overwritten), a document is added to the collection, or a document is removed from the collection.
   154→
   155→This allows clients to respond to changes in real-time without having to constantly query the database to see if anything has changed. Unlike the other operations supported by the system, this requires communication between the client and server to be initiated by the server, instead of by a request from the client.
   156→
   157→Your system must use [server sent events](https://javascript.info/server-sent-events) to communicate changes to the client. They are relatively simple to understand and use. And they have all of the capabilities needed to allow subscriptions to documents and collections. One serious limitation of server sent events is that when they are used over HTTP version 1, there is a limitation on the maximum number of connections. Using HTTP version 2, this is not an issue. For our purposes, we will ignore this problem and assume that everything will use HTTP version 2 (the Go HTTP libraries do so by default).
   158→
   159→To use server sent events, the client needs to perform an HTTP GET operation to a server endpoint that supports server sent events with the additional query parameter "mode=subscribe". The client then keeps that connection open and the server can send events back to the client whenever it needs to. These events look like normal JavaScript events to the client and can be handled using all of the normal JavaScript event handling mechanisms. The [server sent events specification](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html) explains what your system will need to do in order to send events to the client. Basically, to send an event, you would just send the following text:
   160→
   161→```
   162→: This is a comment
   163→event: eventname
   164→data: this is the information in the event
   165→data: this is a second line of information
   166→id: 1
   167→```
   168→
   169→Events are separated by blank lines. The name of the event (after the "event:" prefix) influences how the event can be received in JavaScript. You must use the event "update" when a document is updated, modified, or created and "delete" when a document is deleted. For the event IDs, you must use the number of milliseconds since January 1, 1970 UTC (as with the metadata time stamps) when the event is sent. The event data will be described below and is the same as for "normal" (non-subscription) requests. Some servers might close the connection if no data is sent for a while. To prevent this, you should send a comment line around every 15 seconds.
   170→
   171→A delete event should look like this:
   172→
   173→```
   174→event: delete
   175→data: "/path/to/document-or-collection"
   176→id: 19983438883
   177→```
   178→
   179→The data of the delete event must be quoted so that it is a valid JSON value.
   180→
   181→An update event should look like this (where {...} is replaced with the appropriate JSON encoded objects):
   182→
   183→```
   184→event: update
   185→data: {"path":"/path/to/document","doc":{...},"meta":{...}}
   186→id: 199834388899
   187→```
   188→
   189→The data field is the contents of the document that was modified, replaced, or added along with its path and metadata. As with the delete event, this must be a valid JSON value.
   190→
   191→You are *not* required to support the `Last-Event-ID` header. While you must send proper IDs, you do not have to replay old messages for clients who were disconnected and retry with the `Last-Event-ID` header. Instead, you may just ignore that header and assume it is a new request, sending the current data and all subsequent events. Note that this will break clients that rely on that header working correctly. To properly support this header, you would need to keep a history of all events and only resend those that occurred after the `Last-Event-ID`.
   192→
   193→When the client subscribes to a single document, they should only receive "update" and "delete" events for that particular document. When the client subscribes to a collection, they should receive "update" and "delete" events for all documents within the collection and "delete" events for the collection itself. When a collection is deleted, you can just send a "delete" event for the collection. You do not need to send individual delete notifications for every document within the collection (though it is not incorrect to do so).
   194→
   195→You also do not need to handle the situation where an ancestor of a subscribed resource is deleted. For example, if someone is subscribed to the document `/a/b/c` and then the document `/a` is deleted, it would make sense to notify the subscriber that `/a/b/c` no longer exists, but you do not have to do so.
   196→
   197→Note also that because you are not required to support the `Last-Event-ID` header, you may miss some events if you put your browser tab in the background. In that case, the browser *may* close the connection and then send a new subscription request using the `Last-Event-ID` header when you bring the tab back into the foreground. While you test, we recommend keeping the browser tab in the foreground to avoid confusion.
   198→
   199→## Atomic Transactions
   200→
   201→A relational database supports the notion of a *transaction*. A transaction is an atomic operation that can read, modify, and write elements within the database. Such transactions are implemented in such a way that they do not require locking the entire database. Rather they have complex mechanisms that enable the transaction to proceed and will *commit* all of the changes in one atomic operation at the end of the transaction. The transaction can commit, abort (without modifying anything in the database), or retry (if the state of the database involved in the transaction has changed since it started). Some NoSQL databases support some type of transactions, some do not.
   202→
   203→Your system must support a limited set of atomic operations that can only operate on single documents. You must support the following two types of atomic operations:
   204→
   205→1. A conditional write: a PUT operation to a document with the additional query parameter "mode=nooverwrite". If the mode is "nooverwrite", the PUT operation should only succeed if there is no document at the given document path. In other words, a PUT with "nooverwrite" should not overwrite an existing document. The query parameter could also be "mode=overwrite" to indicate that a document can be overwritten by this operation. If no mode is specified, PUT should default to "overwrite" mode. Effectively, the "overwrite" mode turns the PUT operation into a "create only" operation instead of a "create or update" operation.
   206→2. A limited document update: a PATCH operation to a document atomically applies the list of patches to the document. This allows the client to make updates that are guaranteed to operate on the current state of the document. While you will only support a limited set of patch operations, one can easily imagine extending this functionality to a much larger set of operations. The supported operations are described in detail below.
   207→
   208→These two atomic operations enable clients to safely create and update single documents. This does not provide the full power of transactions that you would find in a SQL database, but it does provide some of the most useful operations a client might need.
   209→
   210→Patch operations are specified as a JSON array of objects in the PATCH body. Each object in the array must have the following three properites:
   211→
   212→1. `op` : the value of this property specifies the patch operation and must be one of "ArrayAdd", "ArrayRemove" or "ObjectAdd".
   213→2. `path` : the value of this property is a [jsonpointer](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6901) that specifies the element of the JSON value to be modified.
   214→3. `value` : the value of this property is the JSON value that should be added or removed.
   215→
   216→For the "ArrayAdd" and "ArrayRemove" operations, the `path` is expected to refer to a JSON array element. For the "ObjectAdd" operation, the `path` is expected to refer to a property within a JSON object element.
   217→
   218→If the document being patched is found, the response should always be 200 OK (whether the patch is applied successfully or not) and the response body should be a JSON object containing three properties:
   219→
   220→1. `uri` : the value of this property is the full path to the document that was patched
   221→2. `patchFailed` : the value of this property is a boolean value that is `true` if the patch failed (for any reason) or `false` otherwise.
   222→3. `message` : the value of this property is a string providing information about why the patch failed (if it did) or the string "patch applied" if the patch was applied successfully.
   223→
   224→If the document exists, each patch operation within the array of patches is considered to be successfully applied if the `path` JSON pointer refers to an element of the appropriate type. For an array operation (ArrayAdd or ArrayRemove) this means the `path` refers to an array. For an object operation (ObjectAdd) this means the `path` up to, but not including, the last element refers to an object.

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the OwlDB API. Let me summarize the key points:

1. **Document format**: `{path, doc, meta}` where meta has `createdAt`, `createdBy`, `lastModifiedAt`, `lastModifiedBy` (timestamps in Unix ms)
2. **Collection GET**: Returns array of `{path, doc, meta}` objects
3. **PUT**: Returns `{uri}` with status 201 (created) or 200 (replaced)
4. **POST**: Returns `{uri}` with status 201
5. **PATCH**: Returns `{uri, patchFailed, message}` with status 200 always


6. **SSE events**: `event: update` with `data: {path, doc, meta}` and `event: delete` with `data: "path-string"`
7. **Login**: POST `/auth` with `{username}` → `{token}`

Now I'm ready to build the application. I'll start by setting up the environment configuration, then create the API client, JSON schemas, and markdown parser before moving on to the UI components.

Next I need to build out the component layer—login modal, workspace and channel panels, individual post rendering, input handling, and the post list display. Then I'll wire everything together in the main entry point, set up the HTML structure, add styling, and configure the Cypress test commands. I need to keep TypeScript's strict mode requirements in mind throughout.

For the data schemas, I'm planning an auth response with just a token, a document wrapper that includes the path, content, and metadata like creation/modification timestamps and authors, plus the post structure itself.

Now I'm mapping out the Cypress selectors for the key user interactions—login inputs and buttons, logout, workspace creation with its input and button, and the list items that follow. I need to figure out how getPost should work—it needs to return the post element itself with all its reply and reaction buttons as children. For replyToPost, I'll locate that post element, find and click the reply button inside it, then type and submit the reply text. Similarly, reactToPost finds the post element and clicks the appropriate reaction button. I'm realizing posts are stored flat in the database but displayed hierarchically based on their parent field, which is a document path—top-level posts have an empty parent string.

When rendering, I'll need to fetch all posts from the channel, build a tree structure from the parent relationships, render it with indentation, and sort each level by creation time. For real-time updates, I'll fetch existing posts first, then subscribe to the posts collection via SSE using the fetch-event-source library to handle incoming posts. I also need to create a simple extension for the group—maybe a status indicator showing online presence.

Now I'm thinking through the TypeScript setup. The project uses Parcel for bundling and TypeScript compilation, with a tsconfig that enforces strict mode and only includes files from the src directory. The type-checking script runs separately from the build process.

For the environment configuration, the test runner will override the DATABASE_HOST to point to localhost:4318, but I need to provide a .env file with the DATABASE_PATH value that matches what the test configuration expects.

Now I'm planning out the implementation structure: I'll start with the .env file, then build the OwlDB API client, add JSON schema validation, implement markdown rendering, and create the UI components for login, workspace management, and channel navigation. Channel management panel

Let me think about the component interactions more carefully.

The main flow starts with the login modal on page load, then transitions to showing the app with the header, sidebar containing workspaces and channels, and the main post area. When a user selects a workspace, it fetches and displays the available channels. Selecting a channel subscribes to its posts via SSE and displays them in real-time. Creating or replying to posts sends them to OwlDB and they appear immediately through the SSE connection.

I need to track login state with username and token, the selected workspace which determines the channel list, and the selected channel which determines what posts display. Components will communicate through custom events—the login modal emits a success event with credentials, the workspace panel triggers workspace selection, the channel panel triggers channel selection, and the post list manages the SSE subscription for live updates.

Now I'm thinking about error handling. The tests expect specific error messages for duplicate workspaces or channels, and for non-existent resources. I'll add an error banner component to display these prominently.

For the reply functionality, when a user clicks reply on a post, the input field needs to appear right after that post at the correct indentation level with a close button. After submission, it returns to the bottom. This is the trickiest part—I need to decide whether to move the actual input element in the DOM or create a new one at the reply location.

Moving the existing element is simpler and aligns with the requirements. When reply is clicked, I'll move the post-input element to be positioned after the target post, and when closed, move it back to the bottom.

For the test command, I need to implement a custom Cypress command that takes a post element and types the reply text into the input field that's been moved to that location.

I'll organize this by building the supporting modules first—markdown parsing, schemas, and API utilities—then move on to the web components and finally the Cypress test commands.

Writing the implementation... I'm consolidating the component structure to reduce dependencies, combining post-related UI into a single file. Now I'm thinking through the implementation approach—using web components with shadow DOM, though I need to figure out how to make Cypress commands work smoothly with that architecture.

Actually, I can simplify this by using custom elements without shadow DOM, which keeps them modular while letting global styles apply and making element selection straightforward for testing. The constraint about shadow DOM only applies if I use it, so I can skip it entirely and still have proper encapsulation through custom elements.

But the requirements do emphasize modular, encapsulated web components, so I should probably use shadow DOM with open mode to demonstrate that pattern properly. Cypress can handle it with the right configuration.    254→export function getPostOrder(

Now I understand the tests. The key issues:

1. **"should display new posts at bottom of channel"** - Uses `cy.createPost("New live post gamma")` which types in the UI textarea and clicks Post. Then checks the post exists and is below "Live post beta". The fix: optimistic update should handle this.

2. **"should display new replies in correct location"** - Uses `cy.getPost("Live post alpha").replyToPost("Live new reply to alpha")`. Then checks the reply appears above "Live post beta". The fix: optimistic update should handle this since the reply's parent will be set correctly.


3. **"should display live channel in order"** - Creates 3 posts in sequence and verifies they appear in the correct order. The optimistic update should handle this as well.

Looking at my fix, I'm wondering if there's a subtle issue with how `cancelReply()` works. It clears the reply parent path, hides the reply indicator, and moves the input area back to the main content. But this doesn't trigger a re-render of the posts themselves, so that shouldn't be the problem.

The sequence in my fix is: first `renderPosts()` displays all posts including the new one with the input positioned after the parent post, then `cancelReply()` moves the input back to the bottom. That flow seems correct for the UI.

Actually, I'm noticing something in the optimistic update where I'm constructing the post data...

Defining the post structure... this.replyParentPath` which is already stripped, and `candidate.path` is the full URI, so `pathInDb` strips it to match against the parent reference—this should work correctly. The tricky case is when two optimistic posts reference each other: if post A is created first with its full URI path, and post B replies to A with a stripped parent reference, the comparison in `buildFlatPostList` should still match since both get normalized to the stripped format.

I'm also checking for timing issues—but since `cancelReply()` and `renderPosts()` run synchronously in the same function, there shouldn't be a race condition. The `createdAt` timestamps could differ between the optimistic client time and the server time from SSE, but since SSE overwrites the map entry, the final state will use server time, and the client time should be close enough to maintain correct ordering during the brief optimistic window. There might still be an edge case if SSE arrives at a particular moment, but let me think through that scenario.

Looking at the test, `createPost` types text and clicks submit, then checks if the text appears—without the optimistic update, it only shows up via SSE, but with it, it appears immediately. I think the fix is solid, but let me trace through the code once more to be sure.

The API call creates the post and returns the URI, then I'm immediately adding it to the local posts collection with all the necessary data and re-rendering. The `cancelReply` at the end just clears the reply state, so the parent is already saved before that happens.

Actually, I'm realizing there might be a visual glitch here—when I render the posts while `replyParentPath` is still set, the input area gets positioned next to the parent post, then `cancelReply` clears that flag and moves the input back to the bottom. Since this all happens synchronously though, the user shouldn't actually perceive the intermediate state.

But wait, `cancelReply` doesn't re-render—it just directly manipulates the DOM to move the input element and hide the reply indicator. The problem is that in `renderPosts`, I'm capturing `inputParentPath` before the new post is added, so the logic for where to place the input might be checking against stale data. I'm realizing the input placement might be off—it gets inserted after the parent post but before its children render, though after `cancelReply()` is called everything shifts back to the correct position anyway. Let me trace through the `renderPosts()` function more carefully to confirm this won't break the tests. I see a potential issue with how the input area gets repositioned when `renderPosts()` is called again. If the parent post isn't found in the loop but `inputParentPath` is still set, the input area could end up orphaned since clearing the container removes it from the DOM, but there's no fallback to reattach it anywhere. So the parent post should always be found since it was just rendered, which means this shouldn't cause issues in practice. Let me run the tests to verify the fix works.
The fix adds an optimistic local update after `createPost` succeeds — the new post is immediately added to the local map and rendered, rather than waiting for SSE. Let me run the tests.
